title: The BEAM Under the Hood
topic: the internals of the Erlang virtual machine (BEAM): schedulers and reductions, per-process heaps and garbage collection, message passing, the process model, distribution, and why these designs make LiveView-scale concurrency work
summary: A runtime-level tour of the Erlang virtual machine for Elixir/Phoenix developers, told in the plain, funny, first-things-first voice of Joe Armstrong. Millions of isolated processes, reduction-counted preemptive scheduling, private per-process heaps with generational GC, share-nothing message passing, the off-heap binary trap, dirty schedulers, and node-to-node distribution — each design choice explained by the failure it exists to survive and the price it charges, then connected back to real symptoms: scheduler collapse, large-binary leaks, and mailbox bloat.

images.alt: A slate chalkboard on which the BEAM virtual machine is sketched as a friendly machine full of tiny glowing processes
images.caption: The BEAM: one machine, a few schedulers, and a great swarm of tiny isolated processes.
images.prompt: A warm, characterful editorial illustration in the style of a hand-drawn chalkboard lecture: a large dark green-grey slate board on which someone has chalked the Erlang BEAM virtual machine as a friendly cutaway machine. A few big cog-like 'scheduler' wheels along the bottom, each feeding a run-queue conveyor, and above them a great swarm of hundreds of tiny glowing round 'process' dots, each a little chalk circle with a short tail, drifting like fireflies. Soft pastel chalk colours — chalk-white line work, warm yellow, sky-blue, mint-green and pink accents — over the deep slate. A dusting of chalk powder along the tray at the bottom. Cross-hatch shading, hand-lettered feel, generous whitespace, no readable text baked in. Target size ~1350x900px, 3:2 landscape; compose with the machine centred and safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 3:2 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.

images.alt: Four sorting clerks at benches, each taking a fixed number of letters from a runner before passing it on
images.caption: A scheduler is a sorting clerk who handles a fixed quota of work, then fairly moves to the next customer.
images.prompt: A warm, homely chalkboard-style editorial illustration: four busy post-office sorting clerks, each standing at their own bench (a 'scheduler'), each with a queue of little customers (processes) holding letters. Each clerk is handling exactly one customer, counting out a fixed handful of letters (reductions) on a little tally counter before ringing a bell and calling the next customer, so nobody waits forever. One customer is being politely waved over from an overloaded bench to an idle one (work-stealing / migration). Hand-drawn chalk look on deep green-grey slate, chalk-white line work with soft yellow, blue, mint and pink pastel chalk accents, cross-hatch shading, gentle humour, no readable text baked in. Target size ~1280x720px, 16:9 landscape; compose centred with safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 16:9 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.

images.alt: Several telephone exchanges wired to each other in a full mesh, passing message envelopes down the lines
images.caption: Distribution: a handful of exchanges, each wired to every other, passing copies of messages down the line.
images.prompt: A warm, nostalgic chalkboard-style editorial illustration: five old-fashioned telephone exchanges drawn as little buildings ('nodes'), each connected to every other by chalk-drawn wires forming a full mesh, with tiny message envelopes travelling along the wires. A small operator at a switchboard in the middle of each building. One wire is drawn as a dashed, fraying line with a little spark to suggest an unreliable network link. A tiny separate booth labelled only with a small port icon hints at a name registry (epmd) without readable text. Hand-drawn chalk look on deep green-grey slate, chalk-white line work with soft yellow, blue, mint and pink pastel chalk accents, cross-hatch shading, no readable text baked in. Target size ~1280x720px, 16:9 landscape; compose centred with safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 16:9 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.

images.alt: One machine holding a vast stadium of tiny glowing processes, each tied by a thread to a distant phone
images.caption: One box, a great crowd of tiny stateful processes — one per live connection.
images.prompt: A warm, awe-inspiring chalkboard-style editorial illustration: a single modest server box on the left, from which fan out hundreds of thin glowing chalk threads, each ending in a tiny round 'process' dot, and beyond them a vast stadium crowd of little phones and browsers — the idea that one machine holds a huge crowd of live connections, one cheap isolated process each. A few dots are mid-shatter with fireflies already reforming them (let it crash, then heal), the rest calm and glowing. Hand-drawn chalk look on deep green-grey slate, chalk-white line work with soft yellow, blue, mint and pink pastel chalk accents, cross-hatch shading, sense of scale and calm, no readable text baked in. Target size ~1280x720px, 16:9 landscape; compose centred with safe margins — it is letterboxed into a 16:9 box, so keep nothing important near the edges.
